content.problemStatement:
There are NNN piles where the ithi^{th}ith pile consists of AiA_iAi​ stones.

Chef and Chefina are playing a game taking alternate turns with **Chef starting first**.

In his/her turn, a player can choose any non-empty pile and remove **exactly** 111 stone from it.

The game ends when **exactly** 222 piles become empty. The player who made the last move wins.

Determine the winner if both players play optimally.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN denoting the number of piles.
Next line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ - denoting the number of stones in each pile.




Output Format
For each test case, output `CHEF` if Chef wins the game, otherwise output `CHEFINA`.


Note that the output is case-insensitive i.e. `CHEF`, `Chef`, `cHeF`, and `chef` are all considered the same.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with an integer N, the number of piles. The next line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN – the number of stones in each pile.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing CHEF if Chef (the first player) wins with optimal play, otherwise output CHEFINA.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: smallest piles are 5 and 10, sum = 15 (odd) → Chef wins. Test case 2: smallest piles are 1 and 1, sum = 2 (even) → Chefina wins.

solution.approach: Only the two smallest piles matter. The game ends when the second pile becomes empty, so at least a1 + a2 moves are performed (a1 and a2 are the two smallest pile sizes). Any move on the remaining piles can be postponed until just before the second empty pile, adding an arbitrary number of extra moves. Hence the players can control the parity of the total number of moves only through the parity of a1 + a2. If a1 + a2 is odd, the first player (Chef) makes the last move; otherwise Chefina does. Therefore, for each test case we find the two smallest Ai, compute their sum, and decide the winner based on its parity.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case (single pass to find two minima). Overall O(total N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space per test case.
